quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Metallic bond"?

options.text: The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed
The electrostatic attraction between delocalized valence electrons and positively charged metal cations in a lattice structure
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest
ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones

quiz_questions.explanation: "Metallic bond" is defined as: The electrostatic attraction between delocalized valence electrons and positively charged metal cations in a lattice structure. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electron sea model"?

options.text: A model of metallic bonding where valence electrons are shared among all atoms in the metal, moving freely throughout the structure
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electron sea model" is defined as: A model of metallic bonding where valence electrons are shared among all atoms in the metal, moving freely throughout the structure. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Alloy"?

options.text: The entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero
A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; ΔH > 0; products are higher in energy than reactants
Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps

quiz_questions.explanation: "Alloy" is defined as: A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Malleability"?

options.text: The ability of metals to be hammered into thin sheets without breaking, due to layers of cations sliding past each other in the electron sea
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C; derived from the autoionization of water: 2H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻
The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single

quiz_questions.explanation: "Malleability" is defined as: The ability of metals to be hammered into thin sheets without breaking, due to layers of cations sliding past each other in the electron sea. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Band theory"?

options.text: The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
E = E° - (RT/nF)ln Q; adjusts cell potential for non-standard concentrations; at equilibrium E = 0 and Q = K
An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
A proton (H⁺) acceptor

quiz_questions.explanation: "Band theory" is defined as: An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The electrostatic attraction between delocalized valence electrons and positively charged metal cations in a lattice structure"?

options.text: Metallic bond
Factors affecting rate
Polyprotic acid
Dipole-dipole interactions

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Metallic bond".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A model of metallic bonding where valence electrons are shared among all atoms in the metal, moving freely throughout the structure"?

options.text: Secondary battery
Electron sea model
Conjugate pair
Fractional distillation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electron sea model".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps"?

options.text: Common-ion effect
Alloy
Stoichiometric coefficient
Green chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Alloy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The ability of metals to be hammered into thin sheets without breaking, due to layers of cations sliding past each other in the electron sea"?

options.text: Malleability
Collision theory
Mole
Second law of thermodynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Malleability".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility"?

options.text: Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)
Band theory
Heat of vaporization
Pressure/volume change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Band theory".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Metallic Bonding and Structures is accurate?

options.text: Increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.
Weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka/Kb values <1.
Metallic Bonding and Structures describes how metal atoms share their valence electrons in a 'sea of electrons' that surrounds a lattice of positive metal cations.
Temperature changes alter K: increasing T shifts endothermic reactions forward (K increases) and exothermic reactions in reverse (K decreases).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Metallic Bonding and Structures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: Concentration expressions include molarity (M = mol/L), molality (m = mol/kg solvent), mole fraction, and mass percent.

This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...
The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Metallic Bonding and Structures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: Alloys (mixtures of metals) modify properties by introducing different-sized atoms.
Orbitals are filled in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...
The rate constant k varies with temperature.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Metallic Bonding and Structures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: The number of bonding pairs and lone pairs determines geometry: 2 groups = linear (180°), 3 = trigonal planar (120°), 4 = tetrahedral (109.5°), 5 = trigonal bipyramidal, 6 = octahedral.
Metallic Bonding and Structures describes how metal atoms share their valence electrons in a 'sea of electrons' that surrounds a lattice of positive metal cations.
Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
If a reaction is reversed, ΔH changes sign; if multiplied by a factor n, ΔH is multiplied by n.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Metallic Bonding and Structures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Metallic Bonding and Structures as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Industrial ammonia production optimizing the ratio of N₂ to H₂ to minimize waste and maximize yield in the Haber process
Copper wiring in electrical systems exploiting metallic bonding's delocalized electrons for excellent electrical conductivity (second only to silver)
Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Metallic Bonding and Structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: Displacement reactions in metallurgy: extracting copper by placing iron in CuSO₄ solution (Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, since Fe is more active than Cu)
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Steel alloys: adding carbon (interstitial) to iron increases hardness; adding chromium and nickel (substitutional) creates stainless steel resistant to corrosion
Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Metallic Bonding and Structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Metallic Bonding and Structures applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Forensic analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to identify and quantify drugs, poisons, and accelerants in criminal investigations
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Gold's malleability allowing it to be hammered into gold leaf just 0.1 μm thick, used in electronics connections, architectural decoration, and aerospace heat shields

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Metallic Bonding and Structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Metallic Bonding and Structures?

options.text: Copper wiring in electrical systems exploiting metallic bonding's delocalized electrons for excellent electrical conductivity (second only to silver)
Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges
Refrigerators and air conditioners: external work (electricity) is required to decrease entropy locally (cool the interior) while increasing total universal entropy

Tempered safety glass as an amorphous solid that, when broken, shatters into small granular pieces rather than sharp shards due to balanced internal stresses from thermal treatment (distinct from laminated glass, which holds together via a...

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Metallic Bonding and Structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Metallic Bonding and Structures in practice?

options.text: Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH
Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Industrial Haber process using iron catalyst to lower Ea for N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ from 230 kJ/mol (uncatalyzed) to ~60 kJ/mol, making ammonia synthesis economically viable
Steel alloys: adding carbon (interstitial) to iron increases hardness; adding chromium and nickel (substitutional) creates stainless steel resistant to corrosion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Metallic Bonding and Structures.
